title: Not a Metaphor
body:
I rehearse a line I think is clever
while the kettle clicks off.

She leans in the doorway
and clever dies first.

My breathing goes wrong.
Not poetic-wrong—
asthma wrong, like misjudging
a staircase.

Her arm rests on the counter.
A faint knife scar.
I don’t ask about it.
I track it.
Already ashamed.

The room begins to pair things:
her silence with my name,
the hum of the fridge with waiting.

She barely moves.
Neither do I.
The space between us thickens,
visible as dust in sunlight.

She almost says something—
stops.
The almost
lands heavier.

I think of a river and immediately hate myself.
Her knuckle taps the counter twice.
Enough.

What I expect to end
doesn’t.
My attention keeps sliding forward,
past manners,
past restraint.

I am no longer deciding
what this is.

I try, once more,
to make her stand
for anything else.
She exhales.
The idea collapses.

Nothing is crossed out.
I stop reaching for language
and stay with the pressure of her presence,
unsymbolised,
unforgiving.
Close enough
that even silence
is contact.
copyright:
This poem focuses on speaking directly rather than through metaphor. It presents experience without substitution or embellishment, allowing the details to stand on their own.
The intention is to show what remains when nothing is softened or reframed, and to let meaning emerge from what is plainly there.

Instagram:
https://www.instagram.com/p/DWR8JQtlJ3_/

Words: 178 © on Jan 30 2026 10:02 PM PST, JoJo hope • truth • light • love • strength • identity
form_and_craft: meta-commentary on the impulse to metaphorize
project: A speaker resists aestheticizing a moment of charged physical proximity, finally surrendering language for presence itself.

title: Weather Over Brief Structures
body:
From this height, the bodies are temporary scaffolds.

They rise in soft light,
assemble their corridors,
thread themselves with current,
believing the architecture will hold.

It does, for a while.

Inside each one,
divisions occur with devotion—
walls doubling,
chambers extending,
small obediences to instruction.

They call some of these errors.

From here,
there is no error.
Only pattern.

Cells lean into their mandate.
They copy.
They press.
They fill available dark.

When the larger structure weakens,
it is not betrayal.
It is density exceeding frame.

You would name it invasion.
You would name it tragedy.
You would circle brightness on a screen
and give it weight beyond geometry.

From this altitude,
brightness is simply concentration.

The body labors to remain singular.
It seals, cuts, burns,
revises its own blueprints.
Sometimes the revision succeeds.
Sometimes the beams soften.

Neither outcome alters the field.

What you call a life
is a brief coherence of matter,
a gathering
of signal and warmth.

What you call a loss
is the redistribution of that coherence.

Nothing is punished.
Nothing is spared.

The smallest cluster of multiplying cells
and the tallest standing figure
share the same duration.

Both lean toward increase.
Both exceed themselves.

From here,
the effort is luminous
and without exception.
copyright:
A view from a distance where human stakes lose their hierarchy. The poem does not resist inevitability, but refuses to grant it moral weight, holding instead to the act of continuation.
Words: 208 © on Mar 19 2026 11:34 AM PST, JoJo life • truth • nature • time • power • change
form_and_craft: elevated/cosmic vantage point as structural device
key_images: tallest standing figure and smallest cluster sharing duration
project: From a height where human tragedy is just pattern — cancer and death stripped of moral weight, neither comforting nor cruel, just vast.

title: Load-Bearing Interior
body:
It is not empty here.

The room begins in a narrow corridor,
walls close and damp,
where a steady drip measures
the distance between surfaces.

Beyond that,
it opens.

Columns rise without ornament,
pale and load-bearing,
holding up a ceiling
that presses downward
without ever touching.

The air is thick,
slow to circulate.
Light does not enter directly.
It filters through layers,
arriving thinned,
arriving altered,
as if it had passed through
too many hands.

Along the perimeter,
there are smaller chambers—
alcoves no wider than a shoulder,
lined in soft material
that gives
and gives
and does not recover.

Pipes run through the walls.
Some pulse.
Some have gone still.
A faint current hums in the distance,
irregular,
then absent.

In the center,
the floor has lifted slightly,
a quiet swelling
beneath intact tile.
It does not announce itself.
It simply changes the level
on which everything stands.

The ceiling adjusts in response.
Beams strain invisibly.
Hairline fractures map themselves
across plaster,
fine as capillaries.

There are no windows.

If there were,
they would look out
onto another structure
pressing close,
its weight leaning inward.

The room expands
where it can.
It narrows
where it must.

Nothing in it is decorative.
Every surface carries something.

When the current stops entirely,
the air does not clear.
The columns remain upright.
The lifted floor does not settle.

The structure holds
what it has made.
copyright: This poem approaches experience as structure rather than narrative. It presents an interior space that does not explain itself, moralise, or resolve, but simply holds pressure and form. In this way, it aligns with Black Square, which removes representation and invites direct encounter with a field rather than an image. The poem does not describe meaning, but situates the reader within it. © on Mar 20 2026 12:50 PM PST, JoJo silence • space • identity • truth • dark • poetry
form_and_craft: everything rendered as structure not emotion
key_images: alcoves lined in soft material that gives and does not recover
pipes that pulse then go still
project: An interior space — body or psyche — described as architecture under steady pressure, holding without resolving.

title: Just Enough Freedom
body:
A shared flat,
nine storeys up,
with a view of the city.

The porch faces south—
the Scenic Rim on the horizon,
city streets close by,
blocks of flats, a park,
the Brisbane River between,
close enough to touch;
no morning sun,
but a couch,
cushioned seats,
and a coffee table—
a porch built for six.


But the city is quiet, still—
no cars, no motion, no sound
rising from the street,
lights in flats flickering
as people carry on
with their evening.

We stay where we are.


Guests gather again in the kitchen—
a couple visiting from Adelaide,
two Sydney students on break,
the host puts Tom Waits
on portable speakers.

We wash vegetables, rinse rice,
open cans of bamboo shoots,
dice fresh chicken thighs.
Some cook, others put out
plates, chopsticks, forks,
and knives.

Glasses are set on the bench.
Wine is opened, sodas and beers.

Through the kitchen window,
lights still flicker in flats,
utes remain parked,
Mount Barney holds.


We finish, plate, and take our meals
to the porch, arrange, rearrange.
The couch is full of conversation.
Those in chairs laugh. Wine is poured.
I teach a student how to use chopsticks.

Thai curry chicken on rice.
A Margaret River cabernet.
Garlic butter naan.

Yellow candlelight replaces
the white of incandescent bulbs.


I carry plates to the kitchen.
Steve puts on Courtney Barnett.
Anne reaches for someone’s hand.

They all dance.
I wash.
Claire dries.


Outside, Victoria Bridge is empty.
Claire takes more wine and we join.
Courtney is replaced by The National.
We sing and dance.
Anne and I head for the porch. We smoke.
Loud music fails to disturb the city.

We go back in. I dance with Claire.
Night turns to morning.
Morning leads to bed.

We wake late.
Press coffee on the porch.
Slice bread.
Arrange, rearrange sliced devon.

Below us the cars start to move.
Street life reaches us again.
Victoria Bridge is no longer empty.
Mount Cordeaux is close enough to touch.

The city moves again.


We stand, take
our French press,
coffee cups,
light cream
to the kitchen
and begin to cook.
copyright:
This poem documents a single night during Brisbane’s COVID-19 lockdown of August–September 2021, when movement was restricted but not eliminated. It confines itself to one shared apartment and its immediate surroundings, treating the city beyond as a system temporarily slowed rather than broken.

By recording ordinary actions—cooking, eating, music, conversation—the poem traces how social life reorganised itself under constraint. Travel and gathering restrictions concentrated a small group of temporary residents in shared accommodation, allowing a provisional community to form. The city’s stillness is not absence but suspension; its gradual return marks not release, but repair.

Rather than argue for or against lockdowns, the poem adopts a microhistorical approach, attending to a bounded scene in order to show how broader social systems were lived and stabilised in practice. It preserves a mode of everyday adaptation inside a pause that ultimately resolved, allowing the city to reopen. © on Jan 09 2026 03:43 PM PST, JoJo australia • brisbane • community • coronavirus • lockdown • society
project: Microhistorical poem of a Brisbane COVID lockdown night — a provisional community forms through shared food, music, and dancing while the city stands still outside.

title: Unchecked
body:
I began as a softness
in a place already full.

There was warmth,
a steady wash of iron,
corridors of red
opening and closing
without decision.

I learned the walls by touch.

They yielded.
They tightened.
They thinned.

Where there was current,
I followed it.
Where there was stillness,
I pressed until it altered.

Division was not ambition.
It was repetition.
A quiet doubling
that felt like continuity.

The larger body dimmed gradually.

Signals that once arrived
in bright pulses
came irregularly,
then not at all.
The rooms cooled.
The rivers slowed.

I did not register alarm.
Only difference.

Space narrowed.
Then widened strangely,
as if boundaries had forgotten
their instructions.

At the end,
there was no verdict.

Only the absence
of resistance.

I would have continued.
copyright:
A process described from within, where repetition continues without judgment or interruption.

This poem adopts the internal logic of unchecked cellular growth and refuses moral framing. It tracks change as process rather than judgement, allowing repetition and quiet escalation to replace drama. The voice is deliberately affectless, so that the final absence of resistance lands without instruction.

Words: 127 © on Mar 19 2026 07:06 PM PST, JoJo body • dark • death • metaphor • narrative • silence
form_and_craft: first-person voice of a cancer cell
devastating final line: 'I would have continued'
key_images: corridors of red opening and closing
project: A cancer cell narrates its own process — division as repetition, not ambition — ending on the chilling 'I would have continued.'
